channel.title: SaaS on Multi-Agent Future
channel.description: Real stories of building AI multi-agent systems — from automated trading bots to AI-managed organizations. No hype, just code and lessons.

item.title: AI Compliance Is a $23B Market — Here's How to Build In It
item.description:
Every Company Using AI Just Got a Compliance Problem

On January 1, 2026, a wave of state AI laws went live across the United States. Texas enacted the Responsible AI Governance Act (HB 149). Illinois started enforcing its amendment to the Human Rights Act under HB 3773, covering AI in employment decisions. Colorado’s AI Act (SB 24-205) takes effect on June 30, 2026, requiring impact assessments that Baker Botts notes “take months to prepare”.
item.content:encoded:
Every Company Using AI Just Got a Compliance Problem

On January 1, 2026, a wave of state AI laws went live across the United States. Texas enacted the Responsible AI Governance Act (HB 149). Illinois started enforcing its amendment to the Human Rights Act under HB 3773, covering AI in employment decisions. Colorado’s AI Act (SB 24-205) takes effect on June 30, 2026, requiring impact assessments that Baker Botts notes “take months to prepare”.

Across the Atlantic, the EU AI Act is ramping into its most consequential phase. Providers of high-risk AI systems must carry out conformity assessments, draw up EU declarations of conformity, affix CE markings, and register systems in the EU database — before placing anything on the market.

And here’s what matters for founders: the companies affected by these laws are not prepared. Most mid-market companies don’t have a Chief AI Officer. They don’t have a compliance team that understands model risk. They’re using ChatGPT in their HR department and have no idea they might be violating Illinois employment law.

That gap between regulation and readiness is a startup goldmine.

The Numbers Behind the Opportunity

The global regtech market was valued at $19.06 billion in 2025 and is projected to reach $23.43 billion in 2026, growing to $105.23 billion by 2034 at a 20% CAGR. But AI-specific compliance is growing even faster — the AI-in-regtech segment is forecast to hit $3.3 billion by 2026 at a 36.1% CAGR.

Those numbers matter because they tell you where the buying intent is. Companies aren’t evaluating AI compliance tools because they think it’s interesting. They’re evaluating them because they have a legal deadline and no internal capability to meet it.

Compare this to the AI chatbot market or the AI writing tool market, where adoption is discretionary. Nobody gets fined for not using Jasper. Plenty of companies will get fined for not doing an impact assessment on their AI hiring tool.

Compliance spending is non-discretionary. That changes the entire sales dynamic.

What the Current Players Look Like (and Where the Gaps Are)

The incumbents in AI governance and compliance are surprisingly few, and most target enterprise:

Credo AI has raised $41.3 million over 4 rounds. Their platform automates AI risk management workflows and maps to regulatory frameworks including the NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, and EO 13960. They sell primarily to federal agencies and Fortune 500 companies.

Holistic AI offers a governance platform that discovers AI systems across an organization, tests for bias and security risks, and generates compliance documentation. They focus on enterprise — the kind of customer that needs to manage hundreds of models simultaneously.

Drata has expanded from SOC 2 compliance into AI regulatory compliance, offering guidance on federal and state AI law programs.

Notice the pattern: enterprise-first, six-figure contracts, long sales cycles, large implementation teams.

Now think about who’s not being served.

A 200-person staffing agency in Denver that uses an AI screening tool. A 50-person SaaS company in Austin whose customer success team uses GPT to draft responses. A European e-commerce brand with 80 employees that deployed a recommendation engine. A mid-size healthcare provider that built an internal triage chatbot.

These companies have compliance obligations under the new laws. They cannot afford Credo AI. They don’t need an enterprise governance platform. They need something more like TurboTax for AI compliance — answer some questions, get your documentation, stay out of trouble.

That’s the gap.

Five Specific Startup Ideas in AI Compliance

1. AI Impact Assessment Generator for SMBs

Colorado’s AI Act requires “impact assessments” for high-risk AI systems. The Baker Botts analysis emphasizes these take months to prepare, which means most companies approaching the June 30 deadline are already behind.

Build a guided workflow — not unlike how TurboTax handles tax filing — that walks a non-technical compliance officer through describing their AI system, its inputs, its decision-making scope, and its potential risks. Output: a formatted impact assessment document that maps to Colorado SB 24-205 requirements, ready for legal review.

Revenue model: $299-999/assessment for SMBs. Annual subscription for ongoing monitoring at $2,400-4,800/year.

Moat: Templates built from real legal interpretations of each state law, updated as case law develops. Every customer’s assessment makes your templates better. After 500 customers, your institutional knowledge of what auditors actually accept is worth more than the software itself.

Execution Prompt:
"I'm building an AI impact assessment tool for Colorado's SB 24-205.
Research the specific requirements of this law — what must an impact
assessment include? What risk categories does it define? What are the
disclosure requirements to consumers? Give me a detailed product spec
for a web app that guides a non-technical user through producing a
compliant impact assessment document. Include the specific data fields,
risk scoring methodology, and output format."

2. AI Usage Discovery and Inventory Tool

You can’t comply with AI regulations if you don’t know where AI is being used in your organization. Holistic AI sells this capability to enterprises, but there’s no affordable version for mid-market companies.

Build a lightweight agent that scans a company’s SaaS stack (via OAuth integrations and API audit logs), identifies which tools have AI/ML features enabled, and produces an inventory. Think: “You have 14 tools with AI features. 3 of them make decisions about people (high-risk under Colorado law). 2 process EU citizen data (EU AI Act obligations). Here’s what you need to do about each one.”

Revenue model: $199/month for continuous monitoring. One-time audit at $1,500.

Moat: The integration library. Every new SaaS tool you catalog and classify (Does Gusto’s AI feature count as “automated decision-making in employment”?) becomes institutional knowledge. After 200 integrations, a competitor would need months to replicate your coverage.

Execution Prompt:
"Design an AI discovery tool that connects to a company's SaaS stack
via OAuth. For each connected tool, it should: (1) detect AI/ML features,
(2) classify the risk level under Colorado AI Act, EU AI Act, and
Illinois HB 3773, (3) flag which features involve automated
decision-making about individuals. Map out the top 50 business SaaS
tools and pre-classify their AI features by regulatory risk category."

3. Employee AI Use Policy Generator and Enforcer

Most companies still don’t have an acceptable use policy for AI tools. Their employees use ChatGPT, Claude, Midjourney, and dozens of other tools daily, feeding them customer data, proprietary code, and personal information. Under the new laws, that’s a liability.

Build a tool that generates a customized AI acceptable use policy based on the company’s industry, jurisdiction, and risk tolerance, then provides a lightweight browser extension or proxy that flags when employees are about to paste sensitive data into an AI tool.

Revenue model: $5-15/employee/month. A 200-person company pays $1,000-3,000/month.

Moat: Policy templates refined by actual compliance incidents. Data classification models trained on what real companies consider sensitive. Usage analytics that help compliance teams understand their actual exposure.

Execution Prompt:
"Create a comprehensive AI acceptable use policy template for a
mid-size company (100-500 employees) operating in Colorado. The policy
must address: approved AI tools, data classification rules (what can
and cannot be input to AI), disclosure requirements when AI is used in
customer-facing outputs, incident reporting procedures, and employee
training obligations. Make it specific enough to be immediately usable,
not a generic framework."

4. AI Bias Auditing as a Service

NYC’s Local Law 144 already requires annual bias audits for automated employment decision tools. Illinois HB 3773 extends similar requirements. Colorado’s law requires ongoing monitoring for discriminatory outcomes.

Most companies doing bias audits today hire expensive consulting firms at $50,000-200,000 per engagement. Build a self-serve platform where a company uploads their model’s decision data (or connects via API), and your system runs statistical bias tests across protected categories, generating audit reports that meet the specific format requirements of each jurisdiction.

Revenue model: $5,000-15,000 per audit. Annual monitoring subscription at $2,000-4,000/month.

Moat: Accepted audit methodologies. Once your reports have been reviewed and accepted by regulators in multiple jurisdictions, that track record becomes your primary competitive advantage. A new competitor’s audit report is unproven; yours has been through the regulatory gauntlet.

Execution Prompt:
"Design the statistical methodology for an AI bias audit that complies
with NYC Local Law 144 and Colorado SB 24-205. Include: which
demographic categories must be tested, what statistical tests to use
(disparate impact ratio, four-fifths rule, etc.), sample size
requirements, confidence intervals, and the specific output format
that regulators expect. Then spec out an API that accepts model
decision data and returns a formatted audit report."

5. Regulatory Change Tracking for AI Teams

AI regulation is moving faster than any compliance team can track manually. New state bills are introduced weekly. The EU AI Act’s implementing guidelines keep evolving. Federal agencies issue new guidance continuously.

Build a monitoring service that tracks AI regulation across all 50 states, the EU, and federal agencies, then automatically maps changes to a customer’s specific AI systems and jurisdictions. Output: “New bill introduced in California that would affect your recommendation engine. Here’s what it requires and here’s your gap analysis.”

Revenue model: $500-1,500/month depending on jurisdictions covered.

Moat: Coverage breadth and interpretation quality. Tracking bills is easy; interpreting what they mean for specific AI use cases requires domain expertise that compounds over time. After a year of interpretation decisions, your database of “what this regulation means for this type of AI system” is the product.

Execution Prompt:
"Build a regulatory tracking system for AI laws. Data sources: state
legislature RSS feeds and APIs, EU AI Act updates from
artificialintelligenceact.eu, federal register entries related to AI.
For each new development, the system should: classify by jurisdiction,
identify affected AI system types, generate a plain-English summary,
and produce a gap analysis template. Design the data schema and the
classification taxonomy for AI system types."

Why This Market Rewards Speed Over Perfection

Here’s what makes AI compliance different from most startup markets: the customers don’t have the luxury of waiting for the perfect solution.

Colorado’s deadline is June 30, 2026. That’s less than four months away. Companies need something now, even if it’s 70% automated and 30% manual. The bar isn’t “better than the enterprise platforms.” The bar is “better than hiring a law firm at $500/hour to manually write impact assessments.”

This is the same dynamic that powered early-stage fintech compliance tools. When GDPR hit in 2018, companies like OneTrust went from zero to billions in valuation not because their initial product was perfect, but because they were there when the deadline arrived. Being present and adequate beats being absent and excellent.

For founders, this means you can launch with a much narrower product than you’d normally consider viable. An AI impact assessment tool that only covers Colorado SB 24-205 is still useful to thousands of companies. You can add Illinois, Texas, and the EU AI Act later. But you need to be in market before June 30.

A Closer Look at What Each Law Actually Requires

Understanding the startup opportunity here means understanding the specific compliance burdens these laws create. Each one generates different product requirements.

Colorado SB 24-205 (effective June 30, 2026): This is the most comprehensive state AI law in the US. It applies to “deployers” of high-risk AI systems — meaning any company that uses AI to make “consequential decisions” about people in areas like employment, education, financial services, housing, insurance, and healthcare. Deployers must complete impact assessments before deployment and annually thereafter. They must implement a risk management policy. They must notify consumers when AI is used in consequential decisions and provide an appeals process. The impact assessment alone requires documenting the system’s purpose, intended benefits, known limitations, data used for training, and an analysis of potential discriminatory impact.

That’s a significant amount of documentation work. For a mid-size company without dedicated AI governance staff, producing this from scratch could easily consume 40-80 hours of a compliance officer’s time — assuming they even know what to write.

Illinois HB 3773 (effective January 1, 2026): Narrower in scope but with sharp teeth. It amends the Illinois Human Rights Act to specifically address AI in employment. Employers using AI to make hiring, promotion, or termination decisions must provide notice to employees and applicants, ensure the tools don’t discriminate based on protected characteristics, and maintain records of their AI systems’ development and deployment. The enforcement mechanism here matters: violations go through the Illinois Department of Human Rights, and the penalties align with existing human rights law — meaning actual legal liability, not just regulatory fines.

Texas HB 149 (effective January 1, 2026): Requires companies deploying “high-risk AI systems” to implement governance programs, conduct impact assessments, maintain human oversight, and provide notice when AI is used in consequential decisions. Texas defines high-risk more broadly than some states, catching AI systems used in criminal justice, employment, education, and financial services.

EU AI Act (phased implementation through August 2026): The most extensive regime globally. Providers of high-risk AI systems must implement quality management systems, maintain technical documentation, conduct conformity assessments, register in the EU database, and enable regulatory access to logs. Importers, distributors, and deployers each have separate obligations. The August 2, 2026 deadline marks when most high-risk AI system requirements become enforceable, with fines up to €35 million or 7% of global annual turnover.

For a startup builder, each of these laws is a product specification. Each required document is a feature. Each deadline is a marketing trigger.

The Defensibility Question

“Isn’t this just a wrapper around GPT that generates compliance documents?” Fair question. And the answer is: it starts that way, but it doesn’t stay that way.

The defensibility in compliance tools comes from three sources:

Regulatory interpretation. When a customer asks “does my chatbot count as a high-risk AI system under Colorado law?”, the answer isn’t in the statute. It’s in the combination of the statute’s text, regulatory guidance, enforcement actions, and legal opinions. Every interpretation you make and get validated (by a customer’s legal team accepting it, or by a regulator reviewing it) becomes institutional knowledge. After a year, your interpretation database is more valuable than the software.

Customer data network effects. When you’ve seen 500 companies’ AI systems and classified them by risk level, you can tell a new customer: “89% of companies using similar AI screening tools classified them as high-risk. Here’s the standard impact assessment approach.” That benchmark data doesn’t exist anywhere else.

Switching costs. Once a company’s compliance documentation, audit history, and risk assessments live in your platform, switching to a competitor means re-doing all of that work. Compliance records need continuity — auditors want to see year-over-year tracking, not a fresh start.

As one Reddit discussion on AI startup defensibility put it: “If you add proprietary data, domain-specific value or deep integration, you’re not ‘just a wrapper’ anymore; you’re a vertical SaaS product powered by LLMs.”

How to Actually Start (This Week)

If you’re reading this and thinking about entering the AI compliance space, here’s a concrete 30-day plan:

Week 1: Pick your jurisdiction and use case. Don’t try to cover everything. Pick one law (Colorado SB 24-205 is the ripest opportunity given the June 30 deadline) and one AI use case (hiring/HR tools are the most regulated and highest-anxiety category). Read the full statute. Read every law firm alert about it (there are dozens from King & Spalding, Baker Botts, Perkins Coie, and others).

Week 2: Build the assessment workflow. Map the statute’s requirements to a step-by-step questionnaire. What information does a company need to provide? What calculations need to happen? What does the output document need to include? Build this as a simple web form — even a Typeform or Google Form works for validation.

Week 3: Get 10 companies through it. Find companies in Colorado that use AI in hiring (LinkedIn Sales Navigator, filter by Denver/Boulder, HR tech, 50-500 employees). Offer to do their impact assessment for free using your tool. The goal isn’t revenue — it’s learning what’s missing from your workflow and getting testimonials.

Week 4: Price it and sell it. You now have a validated tool, customer feedback, and case studies. Set a price ($499-999 per assessment), build a landing page, and run targeted ads to “Colorado AI Act compliance” search terms. The organic search volume for these terms is growing exponentially as the deadline approaches.

Execution Prompt:
"I want to build an AI compliance startup focused on Colorado SB
24-205 impact assessments. Help me create: (1) a complete breakdown
of the statute's impact assessment requirements, (2) a product
requirements document for a web application that guides users through
producing a compliant assessment, (3) a go-to-market strategy targeting
Colorado companies with AI hiring tools, (4) a pricing analysis
comparing my planned $499-999 price point against the alternative of
hiring a law firm. Include specific competitor analysis of Credo AI,
Holistic AI, and Drata."

The Timing Window

Here’s the uncomfortable truth about compliance markets: the window of maximum opportunity is narrow.

Right now, companies are panicking. They have legal obligations they don’t understand, deadlines they can’t meet, and no budget allocated for solutions they didn’t know they’d need. This is when they’ll buy from a startup they’ve never heard of, because the alternative is non-compliance.

In 18 months, the market matures. Large players enter. Law firms build their own tools. Companies hire in-house compliance teams. The panic buying phase ends.

The Y Combinator compliance startup page already shows companies like Ritivel (regulatory submissions for pharma) and Veriad (AI compliance officers for marketing) getting funded in the current batch. The smart money sees the wave coming.

Your advantage as a small, fast-moving founder: you can ship a focused product in 30 days. Credo AI took years and $41 million to build their platform. You don’t need a platform. You need a tool that solves one specific compliance problem well enough that a stressed-out VP of HR will pay $500 to not get fined.

That’s the entire business case.

What’s Next

In the next Startup Ideas post, we’ll break down the infrastructure layer underneath all these compliance tools — the pipes that every AI-powered business needs but nobody’s building yet. Data lineage, model registries, and decision audit trails: the unsexy infrastructure that’s about to become mandatory.

Catch up on this series:

  • Part 1: Vertical AI Agents — Solo Founder’s Best Shot at $1M

item.title: Vertical AI Agents: The Solo Founder's Best Shot at a $1M Business
item.description:
The Agent Gold Rush Has a Counterintuitive Winner

Everyone’s watching the same leaderboard. Sierra at $10B valuation. Harvey AI raising $300M for legal AI. Cognition’s Devin supposedly replacing software engineers. The top 25 AI agent startups have collectively raised over $25 billion.

And that’s exactly why you shouldn’t compete with them.

The counterintuitive opportunity in 2026 isn’t building the next horizontal AI agent platform. It’s picking a narrow vertical — one specific industry with one specific workflow — and building an AI agent that handles it better than any human or general-purpose tool ever could.
item.content:encoded:
The Agent Gold Rush Has a Counterintuitive Winner

Everyone’s watching the same leaderboard. Sierra at $10B valuation. Harvey AI raising $300M for legal AI. Cognition’s Devin supposedly replacing software engineers. The top 25 AI agent startups have collectively raised over $25 billion.

And that’s exactly why you shouldn’t compete with them.

The counterintuitive opportunity in 2026 isn’t building the next horizontal AI agent platform. It’s picking a narrow vertical — one specific industry with one specific workflow — and building an AI agent that handles it better than any human or general-purpose tool ever could.

Here’s the math that makes this work: vertical AI agents show 3-5x higher retention rates than horizontal solutions. They command premium pricing because customers compare them to human labor costs, not software subscriptions. And the biggest players — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google — have zero interest in building a compliance review agent for mid-size dental practices.

That gap is where solo founders win.

Why Vertical Beats Horizontal (Every Time)

The AI agent market is projected to hit $12-15 billion in 2026, growing at a 41% CAGR toward $52.62 billion by 2030. But those numbers obscure a critical structural truth: the market isn’t one market. It’s thousands of micro-markets, each with distinct workflows, regulations, terminology, and buyer psychology.

When Sierra sells to SoFi or Rivian, they’re deploying armies of engineers to customize their platform for each client. Their pitch is “we handle everything.” Your pitch should be the opposite: “we handle one thing, and we handle it perfectly.”

Foundation Capital’s 2026 AI predictions put it bluntly: “The startups that crack enterprise reliability will embed engineers with customers to surface unwritten rules and iteratively improve agents through 1000s of edge cases that only appear in production.” For a solo founder, that sounds terrifying. But flip it around — if you already know an industry, you already have those edge cases in your head.

Here’s what vertical specialization actually gives you:

1. Domain knowledge as moat. The hardest part of building an AI agent isn’t the AI. It’s knowing what the agent should do. A dental insurance claim has 47 different denial codes. A construction safety audit has OSHA-specific reporting formats. A veterinary practice has drug interaction databases that differ from human medicine. These aren’t things you prompt-engineer your way through. They’re things you know or you don’t.

2. Regulatory compliance as barrier. Healthcare has HIPAA. Finance has SOC 2 and SEC regulations. Legal has attorney-client privilege concerns. Every regulation that makes your life harder as a builder makes it exponentially harder for a horizontal competitor to enter your market. Hippocratic AI raised hundreds of millions specifically because healthcare AI needs clinical validation that general-purpose agents can’t provide.

3. Outcome-based pricing. Sierra’s biggest innovation isn’t their AI — it’s their pricing model. They charge for completed work, not seats. When you go vertical, you can do the same thing at smaller scale. Don’t charge a dental practice $99/month for “AI assistance.” Charge them $3 per successfully processed insurance claim. If your agent handles 2,000 claims a month, that’s $6,000 from a single practice — and they’re saving $4,000 compared to a part-time billing specialist.

4. Word-of-mouth in tight communities. Dental practice owners talk to each other. Construction contractors share tools at trade shows. Veterinarians have conferences with 200-person attendance where everyone knows everyone. One happy customer in a tight vertical spreads faster than a $50K Google Ads budget in a horizontal market.

Seven Verticals Worth Building In (Right Now)

I’m not going to give you “AI for healthcare” as a suggestion. That’s meaningless. Instead, here are seven specific workflow-level opportunities that I’ve validated through market research, pricing analysis, and competitive landscape review.

1. Insurance Pre-Authorization for Specialty Medical Practices

The problem: Specialty practices (dermatology, orthopedics, ophthalmology) spend 12-15 hours per week on insurance pre-authorization calls. A full-time employee doing nothing but pre-auth costs $45,000-55,000/year.

The agent: Monitors the EHR for new orders requiring pre-authorization, automatically gathers clinical documentation, submits pre-auth requests through payer portals, follows up on pending requests, and escalates denials with appeal language.

Pricing model: $5 per completed pre-authorization. Average specialty practice processes 400-600/month. That’s $2,000-3,000/month per practice.

Why now: CMS expanded prior authorization requirements in 2025, increasing the administrative burden. Practices are drowning.

Competitive landscape: A few companies target large hospital systems. Nobody is purpose-built for 5-15 physician specialty practices.

Your unfair advantage if: You’ve worked in medical billing, healthcare IT, or managed a specialty practice.

2. Construction Daily Report Automation

The problem: General contractors are required to submit daily field reports documenting work completed, weather conditions, crew sizes, material deliveries, safety incidents, and subcontractor activities. Most superintendents hate this paperwork and do it badly, which creates legal liability.

The agent: Accepts voice notes and photos from the field (via text message or WhatsApp), structures them into compliant daily reports, cross-references against the project schedule, flags schedule deviations, and generates weekly summaries for project managers.

Pricing model: $500/month per active project. A mid-size GC runs 8-15 projects simultaneously. That’s $4,000-7,500/month per company.

Why now: Construction litigation is increasingly won or lost on daily report quality. Insurance companies are starting to require digital documentation for coverage.

Competitive landscape: Procore and PlanGrid handle project management but don’t automate the daily reporting workflow end-to-end. The field is wide open for a voice-first, mobile-first agent.

3. Veterinary Client Communication Agent

The problem: Veterinary practices spend 3-4 hours daily on phone calls for appointment reminders, prescription refill requests, post-surgery follow-ups, and lab result communications. The vet tech shortage (25% understaffing nationally) means clinical staff are burning time on admin.

The agent: Handles inbound and outbound client communications via text and phone. Sends vaccine reminders, confirms appointments, processes prescription refill requests (flagging controlled substances for vet approval), delivers lab results with plain-language explanations, and triages urgent calls.

Pricing model: $800/month for practices under 3 veterinarians. $1,500/month for larger practices. Volume discount for multi-location groups.

Why now: Voice AI agents replacing call centers deliver 60-80% cost savings while maintaining 24/7 availability. Veterinary practices are behind human medicine in tech adoption, meaning less competition and more willingness to adopt a “first” solution.

Your unfair advantage if: You’ve worked in a vet clinic, managed a pet-related business, or have connections in veterinary medicine.

4. Small Law Firm Intake and Conflict Check

The problem: Solo attorneys and small firms (2-10 lawyers) lose 20-30% of potential clients because they can’t respond to inquiries fast enough. When a potential client calls at 3 PM on a Tuesday and gets voicemail, they call the next firm on the list.

The agent: Answers inquiries 24/7 via web chat, phone, and email. Conducts initial intake interviews using practice-area-specific question trees. Runs conflict checks against the firm’s existing client database. Schedules consultations. Sends engagement letters for e-signature. Follows up on unsigned engagement letters.

Pricing model: $1,200/month base + $25 per qualified lead delivered. A firm converting 3 additional clients/month at $3,000 average case value is getting 7x ROI on the tool.

Why now: Harvey AI is focused on BigLaw ($1,000+/hour firms). Nobody is building for the 350,000+ small law firms in the US that run on Clio and have marketing budgets under $5K/month.

Competitive landscape: Legal chatbots exist, but they’re glorified contact forms. An agent that handles the full intake-to-engagement workflow doesn’t exist at the small-firm price point.

5. Restaurant Inventory and Waste Reduction Agent

The problem: Independent restaurants waste 4-10% of food purchased, representing $20,000-50,000/year in lost margin. Most track inventory with pen-and-paper or basic spreadsheets. They order based on gut feel and over-order because running out is worse than waste.

The agent: Connects to POS data to track actual consumption patterns. Predicts demand based on day of week, weather, local events, and historical trends. Generates optimized order lists for each supplier. Tracks actual waste by category. Identifies menu items with consistently high waste ratios and suggests portion or pricing adjustments.

Pricing model: Percentage of documented waste reduction, with a $400/month minimum. If the agent saves a restaurant $3,000/month in waste, you take $600.

Why now: Food costs have risen 22% since 2023. Independent restaurants operate on 3-5% net margins. Waste reduction is the highest-leverage improvement most restaurants can make, and they know it.

6. Property Management Maintenance Triage Agent

The problem: Property managers handling 200+ units receive 50-100 maintenance requests per week. Triaging these — determining urgency, dispatching the right vendor, scheduling access, following up on completion — consumes 15-20 hours weekly. Mistakes are expensive: a misclassified “minor leak” becomes $40,000 in water damage.

The agent: Receives maintenance requests via tenant portal, text, or phone. Uses photo analysis and structured questions to classify severity. Dispatches pre-approved vendors from the property manager’s preferred list. Coordinates access with tenants. Tracks completion and sends satisfaction surveys. Generates monthly maintenance reports by property.

Pricing model: $3 per unit per month. A portfolio of 500 units costs $1,500/month. A property manager with 500 units typically has 2-3 staff members doing nothing but maintenance coordination, costing $80,000-120,000/year.

Your unfair advantage if: You’ve worked in property management, been a landlord, or built anything for the real estate industry.

7. Freight Broker Document Processing Agent

The problem: Freight brokers process 50-200 shipment documents daily — bills of lading, proof of delivery, rate confirmations, carrier packets, insurance certificates. Each document requires extraction, verification against the load record, and filing. One full-time employee handles about 60 documents per day.

The agent: Receives documents via email, fax-to-email, or portal upload. Extracts key fields using vision models. Cross-references against TMS (transportation management system) records. Flags discrepancies. Files completed documents. Generates carrier compliance reports.

Pricing model: $2 per document processed. A brokerage handling 150 documents/day pays $300/day or ~$6,600/month — replacing one $45K/year employee and processing 2.5x the volume.

Why now: Freight brokerage margins are compressed. The industry is notoriously paper-heavy and tech-resistant, which means early adopters get massive competitive advantages over peers still doing this manually.

The Execution Playbook: From Zero to $10K MRR

Picking a vertical is the easy part. Here’s how to actually build and sell.

Phase 1: Validate Before You Build (Weeks 1-2)

Talk to 15-20 potential customers in your chosen vertical. Not “would you use this?” conversations — those are useless. Ask these questions:

  • “Walk me through how you handle [specific workflow] today, step by step.”
  • “What happens when it goes wrong? What’s the cost?”
  • “How much are you spending on this annually — salary, software, opportunity cost?”
  • “If I could automate 80% of this, what would you pay monthly?”

Record every conversation. The specific language they use becomes your marketing copy.

Prompt for customer discovery analysis:

I'm building a vertical AI agent for [industry]. Here are transcripts 
from 15 customer discovery calls. For each call, extract:

1. The specific workflow steps they described
2. Pain points and emotional language used
3. Current costs (direct and indirect)
4. Willingness to pay and price anchors mentioned
5. Objections or concerns raised
6. Technical requirements (integrations, compliance, etc.)

Then synthesize across all calls:
- What's the core job-to-be-done?
- What's the minimum viable agent (fewest steps to deliver value)?
- What's the pricing sweet spot?
- What are the top 3 objections to address in sales?

Phase 2: Build the Minimum Viable Agent (Weeks 3-6)

You don’t need a platform. You need a pipeline. Here’s the stack that works for most vertical agents in 2026:

  • Orchestration: LangGraph or CrewAI for multi-step workflows
  • LLM: Claude 3.5 Sonnet for reasoning, GPT-4o for structured extraction, Whisper for voice
  • Integrations: Direct API connections to 2-3 industry tools (not 50)
  • Interface: SMS/WhatsApp for field workers, web portal for managers, email for document intake
  • Infrastructure: Modal or Railway for hosting, Supabase for data

The key constraint: your v1 agent should handle exactly one workflow, end to end. Not three workflows at 60% quality. One workflow at 95% quality.

Prompt for agent architecture design:

I'm building an AI agent for [specific workflow] in [industry]. 
The workflow has these steps:
[paste the steps from customer discovery]

Design an agent architecture that:
1. Handles each step with appropriate tool calls
2. Includes human-in-the-loop checkpoints for high-risk decisions
3. Handles the top 5 edge cases: [list them]
4. Logs every action for compliance/audit
5. Fails gracefully (what happens when the LLM is wrong?)

Output: system diagram, tool definitions, error handling flows, 
and estimated API costs per workflow execution.

Phase 3: Get Five Paying Customers (Weeks 7-10)

Go back to the people you interviewed. Offer them a deal: 50% off for 3 months in exchange for weekly feedback calls and a case study when you hit results. This isn’t charity pricing — it’s buying data.

Your sales pitch is three sentences:

“You’re spending $X on [workflow]. My agent handles it for $Y. You’ll see results in 14 days or I’ll refund you.”

That’s it. No demo deck. No feature comparison matrix. Cost, alternative cost, guarantee.

Phase 4: Reach Product-Market Fit ($10K MRR, Weeks 11-20)

Product-market fit in vertical AI has a specific signal: customers start referring you without being asked. In tight professional communities, this happens fast. One construction superintendent tells three others at a project meeting. One dental office manager mentions you in a Facebook group with 8,000 members.

The metrics to watch:

  • Logo churn < 5% monthly — if you’re losing more, the agent isn’t reliable enough
  • Net revenue retention > 110% — customers should expand usage over time
  • Support tickets per customer < 3/month — the agent should work without handholding
  • Time to value < 48 hours — if setup takes two weeks, you’ll lose the sale

The Economics of a Solo-Founder AI Agent Business

Let’s model this concretely. Say you build the property management maintenance triage agent (#6 above).

Revenue model:

  • 20 property management companies × average 350 units × $3/unit = $21,000 MRR
  • Annual revenue: $252,000

Cost structure:

  • API costs (Claude + GPT-4o + Whisper): ~$0.15 per maintenance request, 4,000 requests/month = $600
  • Infrastructure (hosting, database, monitoring): $200/month
  • Communication APIs (Twilio for SMS/voice): $400/month
  • Insurance and legal: $300/month
  • Total costs: ~$1,500/month

Result: $19,500/month profit on $21,000 revenue. 93% margin.

This is why bootstrapped AI startups show 3x higher profitability odds in their first three years compared to VC-backed counterparts. When your costs are API calls and hosting, you don’t need $10M in funding. You need 20 customers.

A solo founder running a company with AI agents instead of employees isn’t theoretical — it’s a documented, repeating pattern in 2026. The Business Insider story of a defense-tech founder running his company with 15 AI agents saving 20 hours per week is one example. Dozens more exist in quieter corners of the internet.

What Could Kill This

Let’s be honest about the risks.

Model API pricing changes. If Anthropic or OpenAI dramatically increases prices, your margins compress. Mitigation: build with model-switching capability from day one. If Claude gets expensive, swap to an open-weight model for non-critical steps.

The vertical leader raises $50M and comes for your niche. This happens. But vertical markets have an absorption limit — VCs want $100M+ outcomes, and many vertical niches top out at $5-20M ARR. That’s a fantastic solo-founder business and a terrible VC investment. They’ll leave you alone.

Reliability failures. An agent that processes an insurance claim wrong can cost a practice thousands. Invest in evaluation pipelines, not just prompts. Every agent action should have a confidence score, and low-confidence actions should route to humans.

Regulation. Some industries will regulate AI agents specifically. Healthcare and finance are likely first. Stay close to industry associations and trade groups — they’ll telegraph regulatory changes 12-18 months before they take effect.

The Decision Framework

Ask yourself three questions:

  1. Do I know an industry well enough to list 20 edge cases in a core workflow from memory? If yes, that’s your vertical. If no, spend 3 months working in one before building.

  2. Can I reach 50 potential buyers in 30 days through existing relationships, communities, or cold outreach? If yes, you can validate fast. If no, pick a different vertical where you have access.

  3. Is the workflow I’m targeting costing my customers at least $3,000/month in human labor? If yes, you have pricing headroom. If no, the math doesn’t work — find a more expensive problem.

If you answer yes to all three, stop reading and start building.

What’s Next

Next week, I’ll break down a specific vertical AI agent build from scratch — architecture, prompts, tool integrations, and the first 30 days of customer acquisition. If you’re serious about building in this space, that post will be your technical blueprint.

This post is part of the Startup Ideas series, where I break down actionable opportunities at the intersection of AI and underserved markets.
